data.title: Virtus KAR Mid-Cap Core Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Summary

Broad U.S. equity markets experienced significant volatility in the second quarter, largely driven by the burgeoning trade war. Markets dropped over 10% in early April following the U.S. “Liberation Day” tariff announcements, then surged to new highs by late June as tariff concerns eased among investors. A momentum-driven headwind prevailed, driving lower-quality, higher-volatility stocks to generally outperform higher-quality, lower-volatility stocks—penalizing the kinds of stocks KAR focuses on, which offer the potential for growth with a smoother ride over the long term.

Fund Performance

The Virtus KAR Mid Cap Core Fund (MUTF:VIMCX) returned 6.25% (Class I) in the quarter, underperforming the Russell Midcap® Index, which returned 8.53%. Stock selection in the financials and consumer discretionary sectors detracted from performance, while underweights in the real estate and energy sectors made positive contributions to performance.

HEICO (HEI) and Monolithic Power Systems (MPWR) were the largest contributors to performance during the quarter.

  • HEICO, a technology-driven aerospace and electronics company, continues to execute in all business segments as demand for its parts has increased among both commercial and defense aircrafts. In addition, the company continues to gain placement within the aviation industry.
  • Semiconductor company Monolithic Power, which specializes in power management solutions, reported strong revenue growth driven by design wins in various end markets. The company also provided greater confidence for a recovery in its enterprise data business in the second half of the year.

The Cooper Companies (COO) and Brown & Brown (BRO) were the largest detractors from performance during the quarter.

  • The Cooper Companies is a global medical device company with two business units: CooperVision, a leader in the contact lens industry, and CooperSurgical, which focuses on fertility and women’s healthcare. CooperCompanies’ projected organic growth was lowered by management as the contact lens industry has slowed, however, the company continues to take market share.
  • Brown & Brown is an insurance firm specializing in risk management. Lower organic growth resulting from moderating premium rates led to a reduction in the company’s near-term earnings outlook.

Portfolio Changes

During the quarter, we purchased Advanced Drainage Systems (WMS) and EMCOR Group (EME). There were no complete sales from the portfolio.

  • Advanced Drainage Systems is the leading designer and manufacturer of plastic water management solutions for the stormwater and onsite septic wastewater industries.
  • EMCOR Group engages in the provision of electrical and mechanical construction and facilities services. It operates through several segments: U.S. electrical construction; U.S. mechanical construction; U.S. building services; U.S. industrial services; and U.K. building services.

Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. Please visit virtus.com for performance data current to the most recent month end. Notes on Risk: Equity Securities: The market price of equity securities may be adversely affected by financial market, industry, or issuer-specific events. Focus on a particular style or on small, medium, or large-sized companies may enhance that risk. Industrial Concentration: Because the portfolio is presently heavily weighted in the industrial sector, it will be impacted by that sector’s performance more than a portfolio with broader sector diversification. Limited Number of Investments: Because the portfolio has a limited number of securities, it may be more susceptible to factors adversely affecting its securities than a portfolio with a greater number of securities. Market Volatility: The value of the securities in the portfolio may go up or down in response to the prospects of individual companies and/or general economic conditions. Local, regional, or global events such as war or military conflict, terrorism, pandemic, or recession could impact

the portfolio, including hampering the ability of the portfolio’s manager(s) to invest its assets as intended. Prospectus: For additional information on risks, please see the fund’s prospectus.

data.title: Miran's Case To Cut Federal Funds Rate By 2%: Will The FOMC Buy It?
data.description:
The Fed's new Governor Miran

Stephan Miran, the most recently confirmed Fed governor, gave his first speech to the Economic Club of New York on September 22nd.

Note, Miran has been appointed by Trump, and he is still employed by the Trump administration as a top economic advisor. Thus, Miran's appointment can be viewed as the Administration's attempt to "sway" monetary policy action or to interfere with the Fed's independence.

Consistently, Miran voted for a 50bpt cut at the September FOMC meeting, the only vote against 25bpt cut. In addition, Miran is the most dovish FOMC member, arguing for an additional 2% decrease in the Federal Funds rate.

Miran's case for an additional 2% cut

The FOMC makes the monetary policy decisions by consensus, where the majority is needed to make the decision. Thus, Miran needs to convince other voting members of the FOMC that a more aggressive easing campaign is needed. This is Miran's starting point:

I view policy as very restrictive, believe it poses material risks to the Fed's employment mandate, (and)... I believe the appropriate fed funds rate is in the mid-2 percent area, almost 2 percentage points lower than current policy.

Miran is building his case based on the Taylor Rule and suggests:

The Taylor rule suggests policymakers ought to think about three key variables in determining the appropriate fed funds rate: inflation, the neutral rate of interest, and the output gap.

Miran specifically focuses on the neutral rate r* to build his case:

Because many r* estimates are based on empirical models requiring a great deal of time-series data, they can be backward-looking and slow to adjust. Moving too slowly to update a rapidly changing neutral rate raises the risk of policy mistakes.

Trade policy

Miran argues that the policy changed with Trump's election, and with it the neutral rate. The most important variable in his model is the trade policy, mostly due to the decrease in deficit due to tariffs and the consequent increase in national savings.

The Congressional Budget Office estimates tariff revenue could reduce the federal budget deficit by over $380 billion per year over the coming decade. A 1 percentage point change in the deficit-to-GDP (gross domestic product) ratio moves r* by nearly four tenths of a percentage point. This 1.3 percent of GDP change in national saving reduces the neutral rate by half a percentage point.

In addition, Miran argues that the potential increase in foreign investment in the US due to the threat of tariffs could also decrease the neutral rate.

Loans and loan guarantees pledged by East Asian countries in exchange for relatively low tariff ceilings have reached $900 billion These guarantees entail an exogenous increase in credit supply, which research suggests would be around 7 percent... this would further reduce neutral policy rates by around two tenths of a percentage point.

Overall, he argues, the trade policy could decrease the neutral rate by 0.6-0.7%.

Population growth - immigration policy

The second most important variable in Miran's model is population growth, given the changes in the immigration policy, which could decrease the neutral rate by 0.4%.

It is plausible to me that 2 million illegal immigrants will have exited the country by year-end, thereby reducing annual population growth from 1 percent to 0.4 percent...a 1 percentage point drop in annual population growth can reduce r* by 0.6 percentage point. So, the expected decline in U.S. population growth equates to a nearly 0.4 percentage point drop in the neutral fed funds rate.

Tax policy

Miran also argues that tax cuts from the One Big Beautiful Bill could decrease the neutral rate by up to 0.5% via an increase in national savings and consequently higher GDP.

The CEA calculates an increase in national saving of $3.83 trillion over the next 10 years (relative to the previous policy baseline), resulting from economic growth induced by tax policy. This represents roughly 1.3 percent of GDP, implying a half of a percentage point reduction in r*.

Rents and disinflation

Shifting focus from the neutral rate to inflation within the Taylor rule, Miran argues that the official rents are lagging the falling market rents. This would eventually result in the reduction in inflation from 0.4% to 0.6%, which would be consistent with the Federal Funds rate being 0.5% lower.

Rents for all tenants will fall toward this low rate as people moving or renewing leases sign at current market rates, and I expect CPI rent inflation will decline from its current level of roughly 3.5 percent to below 1.5 percent in 2027. This implies a roughly 0.3 percentage point decline in total PCE inflation; by early 2028, I expect that effect to grow to a 0.4 percentage point decline. Per a Taylor rule, that works out to an appropriate fed funds rate roughly half a percentage point lower than current shelter inflation would imply.

In addition, Miran argues that the tax law is likely to boost output and thus lower inflation via an increase in supply.

The tax law should significantly push out the economy's supply side, an important change to help minimize the potential for inflationary shocks.

Will the FOMC buy it?

The FOMC is data dependent.

Miran has a Ph.D. in Economics, and he found theories (national savings, loanable funds) that could justify the reduction in the neutral rate, but these are only theories and thus completely irrelevant to FOMC decision-making. Each Miran's theory could be challenged with another theory - that's academia.

Miran's arguments are valid - the Trump policies will change the neutral rate, inflation, and output gap. Thus, the Fed will have to adjust the Federal Funds rate. But how, and when?

Powell has been consistent with his view that the net effects of these policies are uncertain. Thus, the FOMC is unlikely to consider Miran's arguments.

Broadly, this is positive for the financial markets, as it demonstrates how difficult it will be for Miran to sway other FOMC members with his arguments, even other Trump appointees on the FOMC.

Implications

The threat to the Fed's independence is one of the biggest risks in 2026, when Trump appoints the new Fed Chair. A more aggressive policy easing could destabilize the US Dollar (DXY) and Treasury Bonds (US10Y). The resulting shock would likely be transmitted to the stock market (SP500).

Based on Miran's playbook, I view the risk to the Fed's independence as very low. The Fed has to remain data-dependent; otherwise, a policy error risk dramatically increases. Miran's theoretical arguments are not appropriate for the FOMC policymaking.

data.title: Quantum Computing: Overvalued And Not Much To Show For
data.description:
High on Its Own Supply

While Quantum Computing might be a promising sector, I personally have significant doubts investing in any of these at this moment in time makes sense.

Quantum stocks seem to have completely lost any feeling of reality, which is partially due to the strong bull market in equities we are currently in. In this article, I will take a deeper look at what is behind the current bull run. In addition, I will explain why I believe these valuations don't make any sense and will suggest ways to potentially play this on the short side without getting burned. Because that's the tricky part in strong bull markets: while a company might be significantly overvalued on a fundamental basis, momentum is a strong thing. Let's take for example, Opendoor Technologies (OPEN) or Oklo (OKLO).

While the above-mentioned stocks are in my opinion not the best investments at this moment in time, they are still significantly better investments than the market's favorite quantum stocks which have seen significant gains this year, mostly due to significant hype around the sector and as such strong momentum.

Now you might ask, why am I writing this article.

Short answer, I have seen a lot of misinformation about the sector and I have seen people who actually understand this sector well and have done significant research on it being completely disregarded. Furthermore, I am of the opinion that shorting these stocks at the right time, could be one of the best trades of the year/years. These will eventually be parabolic short opportunities, but one has to be cautious as short interest is already high and short squeezes happen easily. In addition, borrow rates are often high and puts aren't cheap either.

Furthermore, it is crucial to make sure you don't just short any stock, you look for the weakest one's. Last but not least, I will discuss potential opportunities if you want to invest in quantum computing that have more potential to eventually work out.

The Run-Up

To understand why certain stocks in the sector could become parabolic short opportunities in the future, we first have to understand where the stocks have come from.

For simplicity, we look at the most common names you see online. Ionq (IONQ), D-Wave Quantum (QBTS), Quantum Computing (QUBT), Rigetti Computing (RGTI), Arqit Quantum (ARQQ), and last but not least, the Defiance Quantum ETF (QTUM).

As you can see, a lot of these have run 1000s of percent in one year. Even the Quantum ETF is up 75%, but it is important to note that this has a lot of underlying holdings that have nothing to do with Quantum. As you can see below, there are multiple positions like Intel (INTC), Baidu (BIDU), Oracle (ORCL), that don't have anything (or much) to do with Quantum Computing.

I have to admit that I played IonQ long as well this year, and successfully. I also played QUBT, which was a successful short. But the latter was way more tricky than the long one. That's why it is really important to time them right. It is far easier to be on the long side than on the short side.

I also got pretty lucky with the timing. It was only a small position, as I don't tend to size them large, but a solid week after I wrote about QUBT as a short opportunity. The stock fell 40% in a day as Jensen Huang, the CEO of Nvidia (NVDA), mentioned that (emphasis added):

Quantum Computing is still 15 to 30 years away from being truly useful.

Later, he apologized for this statement.

Valuation & Peer Comparison

So let's take a look at the stuff that really matters, the numbers.

We will start with the two darlings of the quantum investing community, and for good reason, as they are up a ton. Numbers are as of 09/23/2025, based on Seeking Alpha's database.

Ticker Market Cap Revenue (TTM) Net Income / Profitability EV / Sales or Price-to-Sales (P/S)
IonQ ~$21.4B market cap; $52.4 million (TTM) -165M TTM

EV/Sales ~ 399 (TTM)

Price/Sales ~ 313 (TTM)

Rigetti Computing ~$9.2B market cap $7.9M (TTM) -164.8M TTM

EV/Sales ~ 1,108 (TTM)

Price/Sales ~ 893 (TTM)

Quantum Computing Inc. ~$3.72B market cap $0.3M (TTM) -76.4M TTM

EV/Sales ~ 10,970 (TTM)

Price/Sales ~ 9,182 (TTM)
D-Wave Quantum Inc. Market cap ~ $8.9B $22.3M (TTM) -281.5M TTM

EV/Sales ~ 359 (TTM)

Price/Sales ~ 295 (TTM)

I don't think I need to explain why these numbers are extreme. In comparison, if we look at Palantir (PLTR), which is by a lot of people considered significantly overvalued. Palantir has an EV/Sales (TTM) of 122, P/S (TTM) ~ 121.

They still have very, very high metrics, but at least they have been able to grow their business, and they have a use case for their services. This isn't what we currently see with Quantum Computing stocks.

In addition, it is important to note that even the small revenue these companies have isn't even coming from quantum computing most of the time.

What these Numbers Suggest

The common underlying assumption between IonQ, Rigetti, QUBT, and QBTS is that these valuations anticipate incredible scaling, lucrative monetization, and minimal dilution, but today's figures present a dramatically different reality. With revenue bases in the millions (or less) and tens- or hundreds-of-millions losses, the gap between valuation and actuality is massive.

I am not the one who is afraid of investing in companies that are loss-making, but this should be a one-off, or there needs to be a clear track towards profitability. One of my best examples is probably my investment in Hims & Hers (HIMS) early on, which was a loss-making company, but grew significantly and could have easily turned profitable if they wanted to, which they eventually did. I don't have a position in Hims anymore at this moment in time.

When the top line is this small, even modest market capitalizations translate to astronomical P/S or EV/S multiples. That is to say, a small error or delay, all but inevitable in early-stage R&D, can trigger disproportionate negative movement. In the example of QUBT, the gap is huge: hundreds of thousands of dollars of revenues against a multi-billion-dollar market cap. QBTS at least has ~$22M in revenue, but losses are colossus-sized and without a clear path to decrease them, its valuation is premised on hope for rapid, nearly error-free implementation.

This imbalance makes share issuance almost unavoidable. With relentless cash burn and non-profitability, capital raising through equity or convertible securities is the only rational thing to do. Management teams are incentivized to use overvalued stock as currency either to purchase additional runway or acquire revenue-generating assets. For shareholders, then, risk of dilution is not just likely but structural.

Even under the best-case hypothesis, these firms would need to generate an order of magnitude of revenue growth, sustainable relationships, defendable intellectual property, and at least a path to breakeven to justify current multiples. In the absence of that, there is substantial downside risk.

The funny thing is that I believe for the current investors, it would be best for management of these companies to raise as much capital as possible at these valuations and start acquiring solid businesses, as this strategy will actually create some underlying value. But again, current shareholders would be diluted into oblivion in this scenario.

On average growth ratios like the Rule of 40 (revenue growth + profit margin), these stocks are a disappointment. QUBT is disproportionately negative, with no growth and dismal margins. QBTS is far short as well, even with better gross margins, due to massive operating losses wiping out any improvement. In essence, most of these quantum stocks are not acting like high-quality growth names, but as an investment made on "potential hype" while demanding lofty valuations.

Bottom Line On Valuation

The bottom line is simple: the numbers just don't add up. At revenues measured in millions and losses measured in tens or hundreds of millions, these stocks are not worth what their current market price suggests. They can safely be considered as meme stocks.

If investors do want exposure to quantum, the more reasonable route is through diversified tech stocks IBM (IBM), Alphabet (GOOGL), or Microsoft (MSFT) that see quantum as a long-term R&D effort but not their entire business model. I understand these might not sound as sexy, but if there will be long-term winners in quantum it will more than likely be these companies.

For the pure-plays, valuations today are not driven by fundamentals but hope and hype.

The risk to the shareholders is that management groups perceive this gap and respond with the rational thing to do, which is to sell at overpriced levels. Clever corporate finance, maybe, but highly dilutive to existing owners.

Until they can show a credible path to achieving scaling revenue and mitigating losses, they are more hype than reality.

Opportunities and Catalysts

Beneath the mismatch between fundamentals and valuations, there are valid reasons why quantum computing has garnered investor interest. The technology, though nascent, holds revolutionary promise if scaled commercially. A number of catalysts can bring upside to the sector in the long term:

Government and Institutional Funding: Governments consider quantum a strategic technology. The U.S., EU, and China each are spending billions on quantum research, often in partnership with private industry.

For smaller firms like IonQ, Rigetti, or D-Wave, this can mean contracts, grants, or joint research that offers some offset to operating costs and helps validate technology. Continuing funding support is one of the few reliable near-term sources of revenue.

Big Tech partnerships: Microsoft, Google, Amazon (AMZN), and IBM have each invested in quantum research. Pure-play quantum firms usually ally with these giants to match hardware with cloud services or to jointly develop applications. These alliances don't guarantee commercial success but do provide credibility and can accelerate adoption. IonQ's integration with Microsoft's Azure Quantum, for example, is a case in point. But again, while this would be the way to go for the pure-plays, we haven't seen much of these cases yet as the big boys (the Microsofts and Googles of this world) know that they are better off trying to develop it themselves, at least at this moment in time.

Commercialization Pathways: The sector is beginning to pilot real-world use cases, such as optimization problems, pharma, and logistics. Revenues currently are de minimis, but proof-of-concept wins, even small contracts with corporates or research institutions can stir up investor interest. Demonstrating clear use cases is one of the single most important near-term catalysts. But again, this is a significant if. So far, the actual use cases have been extremely limited, not to say non-existent.

First-Mover Advantage: Early credibility matters in deep-tech industries. Those who can position themselves as the "default" platform in the minds of enterprise clients can capture a disproportionate share of contracts and partnerships. IonQ, for example, has benefited from being thought of as one of the more advanced pure-plays despite having relatively modest revenue. Nevertheless, a lot of the big players have been working on Quantum for decades without any significant breakthroughs. Will one of the pure plays succeed in creating an actual use case? Time will tell, but most of them more than likely won't.

Speculative Capital Cycles: Finally, do not discount the speculative angle. Quantum computing is something obscure, it is a tough topic that a lot of people fail to grasp. This type of topic creates a narrative that stirs retail passion once these stocks see a bit of momentum. Just like EV or AI stocks trade well ahead of fundamentals, quantum names may just continue to rally on story alone whenever there is positive industry news. This also is often seen by the kind of cult following these stocks are able to get. IONQ, for example, has quite a solid base on X (formerly Twitter).

Traders who enjoy this dynamic can seek opportunities, which I would even highly consider if you are a momentum trader. If you are a trader and haven't been in Quantum stocks this year you are missing out, even if long-only investors are not convinced.

The Tech is Still Years Out

Normally, I would provide a risk section in an article. However, I think I have outlined the risks for both long -and short-oriented investors already. As such, it is more useful to look into some of the reasons why there isn't a clear pathway to profitability for these stocks, which isn't the case for the other hot topic, namely AI, which has been widely adopted and has proven use cases.

Multiple independent sources from the U.S. National Academies to IEEE Spectrum conclude that scalable, fault-tolerant quantum computing remains years away, likely extending into the 2030s for meaningful commercial impact.

In addition, it is clear that the management of these companies doesn't believe it themselves, as every acquisition done by these pure-plays is done in all-stock deals. Which, in my opinion is the right thing to do at these valuation levels, but for an existing shareholder, this doesn't really instill confidence. A good example here are QUBT's $500M Private placement and IONQs $1.3B worth of deals which they mostly paid in shares. We can clearly see that these management teams are using equity as currency.

Multiple other analysts are being Skeptical

I am glad to see that other analysts on this platform are also skeptical. Meanwhile, I have also seen other analysts on here, who I consider good analysts, being very bullish on some of these stocks. I guess it is more than likely engagement farming, as they are well aware that these stocks are trading on pure hype.

One analyst who has been skeptical, and rightfully so, is Stone Fox Capital. This is a long-time SA author who has been warning about Quantum stocks. Of course, the price hasn't moved in his favor, but I believe he will eventually be right. That said, timing is everything on these parabolic shorts as I discussed earlier. One has to wait and strike when the opportunity arises.

Another person I consider a worthwhile follow, although being a controversial figure is Martin Shkreli. Yes, some of you might know him better as "pharma bro." Shkreli is a brilliant biotech analyst, with big winners for example his recent short on aTyr Pharma (ATYR), but he also knows a lot about technology and Quantum Computing.

Skhreli has built significant short positions, namely in IONQ. While he is not always right (nobody is), he is a loud but credible voice online about how dumb the valuations are.

We can go much further on the case against these companies, but that is not the goal of this article. This article had the goal of showing why investing in pure-play quantum stocks might not be smart at the current valuations. In the future, we might do a deep dive on individual companies mentioned in this article, but I think it is already clear why these stocks are potentially one of the best short opportunities out there once the market turns.

We can go much further on the case against these companies, but that is not the goal of this article. This article had the goal of showing why investing in pure-play quantum stocks might not be smart at the current valuations. In the future, we might do a deep dive on individual companies mentioned in this article, but I think it is already clear why these stocks are potentially one of the best short opportunities out there once the market turns.

Conclusion

Quantum computing as an investment opportunity remains high-risk with not much to show for.

The public pure-plays are presenting this picture in broad daylight: billions of dollars worth of valuations, millions (or tens of thousands) of dollars of revenues, and burn rates that essentially guarantee shareholders will get diluted. By almost any conventional measure, these firms are worth less than their share prices suggest.

That is not to conclude the potential is illusory. Governments are putting billions of dollars into quantum research, big tech firms are forging partnerships, and pilot programs are beginning to dip into real-world applications. The ultimate reward, if it's achieved, will be enormous. But in between here and there lies a long runway of skepticism, technological hurdles, and budget constraints.

To investors, the message is clear: the majority of these stocks are not an investment, they are a bet. Traders may perhaps be able to spot opportunities as these stocks are highly volatile. In addition, when the hype cycles comes to an end shorting these stocks will be one of the best opportunities in year(s).

If you wish to have exposure to quantum, the safe route is likely to be via diversified tech leaders like IBM, Microsoft, or Alphabet, who can invest in quantum within overall R&D budgets without putting the business on it.

Until quantum computing companies can bring in hefty revenues and show a viable path to profitability, they are essentially venture-stage science experiments offering on public stock exchanges. The tech could change the world but it doesn't mean today's quantum stocks will reward investors.

As such, I consider the sector as a whole as overvalued. Long-term investors in these stock should be very cautious.

data.title: Western Digital: Spins Too Hot
data.description:
Intro

Founded in 1970, Western Digital Corporation (NYSE:WD) is a dinosaur of the computer era. It evolved into a global leader in data storage, initially dominating hard disk drives, or HDDs in brief, and later expanding into the flash business through its 2016 acquisition of SanDisk (SNDK), which was spun off again in February 2025 to sharpen focus and strengthen its balance sheet.

Important to know, HDDs use spinning disks for high-capacity storage but generate more noise and heat. SSDs on the other hand use flash memory for faster, quieter, and cooler operation with lower capacity. The latter is more and more used in consumer electronics, while HDDs experience high demand from sources where high storage capacity is needed at low cost, and where data does not need frequent and quick access back and forth. HDDs are slower than flash drives.

Far from a dying legal entity, WD thrives despite SSD and flash growth, leveraging a 6x cost advantage in HDDs (80% of storage capacity) and projecting 15–23% exabyte growth through 2028, driven by AI demand. Exabyte comes after petabyte which again comes after terabytes, i.e. an exa is 1,000,000 tera. The company innovates with high-capacity drives (32–44 terabytes, highest spec MacBook Pro has 8 TB flash), and maintains pricing power via strong hyperscaler demand. This is boosting margins through price hikes, efficiencies, and comparatively low required investments.

WD stock has seen a phenomenal run as of late. However, I am very cautious when shares go almost vertical. WD's valuation warrants caution.

Buying WD stock now could byte into your portfolio

WD is clearly riding a digital wave, capitalizing on soaring AI-driven storage demand, as highlighted by CFO Kris Sennesael at the Goldman Sachs Communacopia + Technology Conference 2025.

As a bonus on top, the storage industry as a whole is powering through a tight supply-demand environment, driven by robust AI-fueled storage demand, as outlined by the CFO. Although personally I also initially thought that AI training would require fast responses and would be favoring flash storage as a consequence, there's a difference between data that is "cold stored" and "warm stored". The former is not accessed frequently, while the latter is. This lets the operator using HDD especially for cold stored data to save on costs.

WD projects a 15–23% CAGR in exabyte growth from 2024 to 2028, leaning toward the higher end as AI adoption accelerates. Western Digital sees supply as tight but manageable, primarily through areal density improvements on newer disks. By increasing drive capacities (from 32 to 36–44 terabytes, same size or only marginally bigger, but more storage per space) and shifting the average capacity per unit from 22 terabytes toward higher ends, WD meets demand without expanding unit capacity. This is important to not overproduce and crash prices again.

Operational efficiencies, including yield improvements (higher-quality output), automation, and AI-driven manufacturing, further boost output while maintaining CapEx at 4–6% of revenue. As a mature company, and despite innovations, HD does not have high Capex requirements, allow for healthy margins. Here are gross margins which are on the rise:

And here we have cash flow and Capex. Note that the more capital-intensive flash business via Sandisk has been spun off only recently, so going forward, WD should be able to generate healthy cash flow from operations, but also after FCF after Capex.

Pricing currently remains stable on a per-terabyte basis which at first sight sounds like bad news as demand outweighing supply should boost prices. However, this is a stark contrast to historical 7% annual ASP (average selling price) erosion – yes, storage got cheaper and cheaper – so this is effectively a win. This is further supported by long-term agreements and purchase orders with four of its five largest customers through FY26, as explained during the conference. Speaking of biggest customers, the three biggest customers account for 17% of total sales. This is not an extreme dangerous dependency, but this concentration has been on the rise over the last years. Just something to have an eye on.

The company’s cloud business, now 90% of revenue, overshadows crumbling client and consumer segments (10%, ~$1 billion, the business lost to flash), so effectively WD has become an AI story.

Western Digital’s massively improved gross margin is a significant leap from the 20s a few years ago, driven by stable pricing, cost efficiencies, and a favorable mix toward higher-capacity UltraSMR drives (40–45% of nearline shipments, targeting 50% by year-end 2025). WD's build-to-order strategy, backed by tight customer partnerships, minimizes overshipping and inventory overload risks, with some customers holding less than a week of stock even.

Thanks to a 6x cost advantage over SSDs in acquisition and 3.6x in total cost of ownership, HDDs retain ~80% of storage capacity share, even in new installations. So, we have an intact business model.

Financially, the company strengthened its balance sheet post-February 2025 separation, reducing net debt to $2.6 billion (now c. 1x EBITDA).

All in all, this sounds like a great long-term story. With hyperscalers driving 68% of revenue, Western Digital is well-positioned to store the future.

But the stock’s high valuation warrants caution.

Valuation – into the clouds

The performance over the last ten years is not even that spectacular. But the playing field has changed substantially, with AI being a relatively new growth narrative. While it is of course possible that on an operational basis the company continues to thrive, I have started to get crumblings in my stomach for other reasons.

The almost vertical move for such a heavyweight over the last not-even half year is the starting point.

Being fully aware of the strong pricing power and demand situation, the truth is, there comes a point in time where all this is already known accordingly priced into a stock.

Looking at price to sales, this does not look conservatively valued. The trough for this highly cyclical business (please don't forget that!) can be below 1x sales during a downswing. It does not have to, but being above 4x offers substantial room for a painful correction.

Higher profitability warrants a higher sales multiple, okay. Let's look at free cash flow. With net debt being minimal, the EV is now around $41 billion. FCF for the next year is expected to be $2.4 billion, a new five-year high. I think this is achievable, supported through higher margins and having got rid of lower-margin Sandisk. The result is an EV / FCF of 17x.

This does not sound very high at first glance.

The problem is WD is now in an unprecedented period of price stability for its drives, while the norm in the past was falling storage costs. We discussed 7% per year above. Let's also not forget that we are dealing with a cyclical industry that sooner or later will go through a painful correction.

Yes, maybe WD stock will make another 50% first up. But I see much more risks to the downside. That's why WD for me is a sell, as moves in both directions can be very violent. I don't know when the next correction comes. But I would not want to be caught on the wrong foot, risking a 50% downside (which would still not make the stock extremely cheap!), while the upside seems limited, absent of emotional exaggeration.

Risks to my Thesis

Greed and optimisms usually last longer than panics. That's why the stock could continue rising.

With stronger cash flows, management could start to buy back more aggressively its own stock, keeping upwards momentum for the stock.

Any unexpected new innovation could cause another boost and more optimism.

Conclusion

Western Digital seems to be spinning too hot right now. While the operational business has strong tailwinds, let's forget this is a highly cyclical sector. The valuation is based on elevated hopes and an unprecedented environment of not falling storage prices. I am advocating to take the chips off the table.

data.title: Vista Gold: A Rare Precious Opportunity In Its Sector
data.description:
Intro

Vista Gold Corp. (NYSE:VGZ), is a gold developer. It shines with its Mt Todd project in Australia’s prestigious Tier-1 jurisdiction. Mt Todd is the company's sole project.

While many gold mining stocks have experienced strong runs, especially this year, thanks to a high gold price (above $3,700 as of writing) and multiple expansion, many names are already priced for perfection, making more upside depended on even higher gold prices (which I do not rule out, but I need internal drivers).

Vista's undervalued asset, nestled near Darwin, Northern Australia, boasts a huge 5.2 million ounces of proven and probable reserves, with potential for further increases through the conversion of resources. The recently updated feasibility study (FS) from July 2025 has transformed its outlook, positioning Vista for a significant re-rating. With permits (read: updates to existing permits) nearing approval within 12-18 months and strategic options like partnerships or a takeover on the horizon, the upside glimmers brightly. Management’s openness to financing, partnering, or selling hints at a potential gold rush for investors, under the assumption the price of gold does not fall into the pit.

Shifting into a lower Gear to speed up

Vista Gold’s strategic pivot, detailed in the July 2025 feasibility study, marks a game-changing downsizing of the Mt Todd project. This could have resulted in disappointment, but the reaction was positive, as this necessary step de-risked the project and brought forward the next steps.

As background information, previously, the 2024 study projected exorbitant upfront costs of a billion USD, making self-development unrealistic. As a reminder, not too long ago, the single-project company had a market cap in the low $100 million sphere. The new plan slashes initial Capex by 59% to $425 million, which is still not just spare change money for the company, but it makes the development significantly easier. On the other side, this step reduces expected yearly gold output by two-thirds to a manageable 150,000 ounces.

I fully support this strategy shift, as it would not have made sense to box in the wrong weight class. This rightsizing focuses on quality over quantity, ensuring consistent production over a 30-year mine life rather than a quick depletion, making for a more compelling long-term story.

Production costs, revised upwards, now estimated at $1,400-$1,500 per ounce, align with industry averages, preserving healthy margins even at conservative gold prices. At first sight, it is negative that estimated production costs went up by almost 50%, but the end result is still strong enough to offer an interesting investment case in my opinion.

This shift strongly de-risks the project, making it attractive for development, being it self-financing or partnerships. During the last call, management pointed out that they're open to all solutions, given that it is attractive for shareholders. Self-development is possible, the same if a partner will be brought in, but there could also be a full sale in the cards.

Anyway, with a strongly supportive gold price, this stock should continue climbing until it closes its significant valuation gap. Below is a chart showing that Mt Todd is not just another little gold project. However, its valuation is very low due to, of course, not being in production and with financing details yet to be announced. Since the deck was published, VGZ's stock more than doubled, but the golden bubble would still not have moved up much.

A standout strength is Mt Todd’s location in Australia, a Tier-1 jurisdiction renowned for its political stability, robust infrastructure, and mining-friendly regulations. This reduces operational risks, offering investors (small shareholders, but also potential acquirers) a relative safe haven compared to less predictable regions in emerging markets.

The updated study’s emphasis on higher-grade ore enhances profitability, while the existing permit framework – needing only minor updates – streamlines progress.

Below, we can see that, once shovel-ready, Mt Todd starting from the second year of operation would pour out practically the company's current market cap in FCF in a single year – with an assumed gold price of $2,500.

Despite the downsizing, or maybe because of it, the project’s economics remain solid. This, paired with a resource base twice the reserve size (!), positions Vista as a long-term contender, given its project's size.

With a current market cap of ~$280 million USD, Vista Gold’s valuation still sparkles with opportunity. The Mt Todd project’s net present value ranges from $1.1 billion at $2,500/oz to $2.1 billion at $3,300/oz, suggesting a potential 4-7x upside. I deem this highly lucrative in a market where expectations are already high.

A look at the financials (latest quarter report) shows a cash position of $13.5 million, and total current assets of $15.1 million.The asset base is currently mainly cash.

Current liabilities and even total liabilities are less than $1.3 million. Cash burn over the first six months of the year was $4.1 million on an operation level.

In essence and at the current pace, the cash runway, all else equal, just theoretically would be about 1.5 years. Of course, management won't drain reserves to zero, and after having announced to be both open for financing to start construction on its own, but also via a partner, over the next twelve months we should likely see a new dynamic. The first critical step was the right-sized feasibility study. The new plan is now clear.

What I would also keep an eye on and keep in mind is that the gold price has risen by more than 10% since the updated FS was published. This makes the project more valuable on a spot rate basis. This could cause FOMO in potential partners or acquirers, so I guess over the next 6–12 months no stone will be left untouched.

Risks to my Thesis

There's no guarantee a partner will be found. The same for a potential sale of the company. On the other hand, financing could be given only at unfavorable terms, erasing some of the valuation potential. Also, if the company raises equity, shareholders will be diluted. Depending on the scope, the reaction could be negative.

Conclusion

I believe the downsizing of the Mt Todd project isn’t a retreat but a clever recalibration, turning a potential white elephant into a golden goose. Vista Gold is a rare opportunity in a sector of high expectations.

data.title: General Motors: Stay On The Sidelines, As Tariff/ Cash Flow Risks Mount
data.description:
GM's Rally Occurred Overly Fast & Furious, Given The Profitability Risks Arising From Tariffs Uncertainties & Higher Capex

We previously covered General Motors Company (NYSE:GM) in July 2025, discussing how the ongoing tariff war was expected to trigger up to -$5B in adj. EBIT impact, worsened by the potentially lower free cash flow generation arising from the moving of its manufacturing capacity to the US over the next two years.

While the management had offered numerous tariff mitigation strategies, we had believed that price hikes might be unavoidable, with it likely triggering further consumer affordability issues as borrowing costs remained elevated.

Given the rising structural risks, we had preferred to continue rating the stock as Hold then, despite the supposedly cheap stock prices and the robust shareholder returns thus far.

GM 1Y Stock Price

Since then, the bulls have already proven our bearish thesis wrong, with GM rallying by +10.9% compared to the wider market at +6.1% and a similar outperformance also observed in some of its automaker peers in varying degrees.

On the one hand, part of the recovery tailwinds may be attributed to the automaker's robust US sales growth by +7.3% YoY in FQ2'25 and the expanding overall market share to 17.4% in H1'25 (+1.2 points YoY), along with the higher EV sales in August 2025 and the likely to be robust EV sales in September 2025 (before the EV credit expiry).

On the other hand, while GM may have outperformed the industry wide growth at +2.7% YoY and Stellantis (STLA) at -10% YoY over the same time period, it still underperforms its direct competitor, Ford (F) at +14.2% YoY, with it highlighting why the former's recent recovery may have occurred overly fast and furious.

This is especially the management has highlighted a rather hefty annualized gross tariff impact of up to $6.7B, based on the 3 quarterly impact at between $4B and $5B.

Part of the bottom-line headwinds may be attributed to GM choosing to continue importing from "Canada, Mexico and Korea to avoid interruptions for our customers and dealers" and China for EV batteries, pending the completion of its manufacturing capacity expansion to over 2M and the realization of its ongoing battery joint venture in the US by 2027.

This is on top of the eventual offsetting efforts by at least 30% through "strategic actions such as manufacturing adjustments, targeted cost initiatives and consistent pricing," with it implying an eventual net tariff impact of ~$4.69B.

These reasons are also why we urge readers to temper their expectations, since GM is expected to report their FQ3'25 earnings call sometime in October 2025 (official date yet available).

Our caution arises from the fact that the tariffs may drastically impact its FQ3'25 performance despite the robust August 2025 sales, with FQ3'25 deliveries likely being an outlier and FQ4'25 bringing forth painful moderations from the +100% EV sales growth observed in FQ2'25.

The same has been observed in the estimated H2'25 adj EBIT performance at $4.73B (-28.2% YoY) and adj EPS at $3.815 (-22.4% YoY), as hinted by the GM management's reiterated FY2025 guidance in the FQ2'25 earnings call.

We are likely to see an impacted automotive Free Cash Flow performance as well, attributed to the ongoing retooling in its manufacturing capabilities from EV to gas platforms along with the battery joint ventures, down from the $14B reported in FY2024 (+19.9% YoY) and $3.63B in H1'25 (-43.1% YoY).

While these capex investments are expected to drive GM's expanded manufacturing capabilities within the US while eventually reducing the tariff impacts, it is apparent that the changing policies and the fluctuating consumer demand have been detrimental to its planning processes.

These headwinds have rendered the automaker's prior re-tooling of its manufacturing capabilities from ICE to EV platforms in 2024 useless, while underscoring their delayed monetization path.

Depending on the results of the next election cycle, we believe that there remain great risks to the legacy automaker's execution, since it is uncertain how the regulatory landscape may shift ahead.

The one silver lining to GM's investment thesis will be its healthy balance sheet at a net cash position of $12.14B (+2.4% QoQ/ -16.2% YoY), allowing them to mitigate the near-term headwinds over the next few quarters before normalizing from FY2027 onwards.

This is on top of the robust shareholder returns with -14.9% of its shares already retired over the LTM and -31.7% since FY2019, with it underscoring the excellent use of its rich cash flows along with the ongoing expansion of its book value per share to $69.34 (+11.1% YoY/ +132.2% from FY2019 levels of $29.85).

So, Is GM Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

GM 5Y Stock Price

For now, GM has already charted an eye-watering rally by +38.8% since the April 2025 bottom and +121.3% since the November 2023 bottom, with it apparent that the bullish support has brought the stock nearer to the book value per share as discussed above and our fair value estimates of $57.10.

This is based on the management's FY2025 adj EPS guidance of $9.125 at the midpoint (-13.9% YoY) and the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 6.26x (not too far from its 10Y mean of 6.69x).

Thanks to the massive stock price gains by +73.4% since our prior Buy rating in June 2023, it is apparent that there is a minimal margin of safety to our long-term price target of $64.40, based on the consensus FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $10.30, assuming that GM's cost savings strategy and tariff mitigation efforts work out as expected.

In a direct contrast to F's rich dividend investment thesis at forward yields of 6.45%, GM does not offer a compelling income yield at 1.02% compared to the sector median at 2.42%, despite the last raise by +25%.

GM's Nearing Overbought Levels

At the same time, GM is nearing overbought levels by the time of writing, with it remaining to be seen if the stock will be able to break out of the established resistance levels of $60s, barring which, the stock may very well retrace to its intermediate support levels of $50s ranges.

This conjecture is not overly bearish as well, since it remains to be seen what FQ4'25 may bring for the automaker's performance metrics and the consumer demand for its auto offerings, as the EV credit expires from October 2025 onwards.

These headwinds may be potentially well balanced by the recent Fed rate cut and the cheaper borrowing costs compared to the 7% observed in August 2025, along with the a speculatively upgraded guidance attributed to the developing trade negotiations between the US/ Mexico/ Canada/ Korea and the locked-in material prices.

Otherwise, given the minimal return prospects across capital appreciation/ dividend income at current levels, we prefer to stay on the sidelines while maintaining our Hold rating for the GM stock here.

data.title: SES AI Acquires UZ Energy And Profits On Molecular Discovery AI Platform
data.description:
SES AI (NYSE:SES) has entered the revenue stage and reports further synergies with the acquisition of UZ Energy. The company is gaining momentum with its AI molecular discovery platform as used to innovate EV battery cells. The company’s stock price has also gained momentum. The price has increased 180% over the last 12 months and 215% over the last 6 months. The price is up 74% since I covered it on Seeking Alpha last April.

The company recently released its Q2-2025 results, which mark the third quarter of reporting revenues. The company’s stock price is overvalued and trades at more than 2x its book value. Investor sentiment is positive on the company and its business strategy. It will take time for the company’s financial performance to reach net income and free cash flow. There is moderate to high risk in holding the stock because future earnings and performance are uncertain. The company has plenty of liquidity to continue and expand its operations. I continue my rating of a Strong Buy for the company.

Current Synergies

SES AI has completed its acquisition of UZ Energy, which specializes in energy storage systems (ESS). UZ Energy is a Chinese company with operations in Australia, China, Europe, Japan, and North America. SES AI will integrate its AI platform with UZ Energy’s existing ESS operations. The company expects an increase in revenues from UZ Energy’s operation.

SES AI described its molecular discovery AI platform in its last Q2 earnings call. The company has launched its Molecular Universe, which consists of a chatbot and AI agent, both trained in computational chemistry and battery-relevant molecules. The system will find the best molecule for the particular battery need. The knowledge also includes existing research and statistics on battery performance. The company reports that over 30 companies have begun using its platform. The platform is licensed to companies and researchers who are interested in developing better EV batteries. There is also demand from manufacturers of drones and humanoid robots.

SES AI is currently implementing further functions of its AI platform. The company says the next step in the development of its system concerns the integration of the AI Molecular Universe with physical working systems, including EVs, drones, robotics, and energy storage systems (ESS). The company foresees the integration of AI directly into battery systems for the purpose of monitoring the battery and its health, as well as suggestions for improvement. With its recent acquisition of UZ Energy, SES AI will use its AI system to monitor the storage systems and will sell energy storage monitoring services.

The company has several strategies for revenue growth, many of which are already in motion. Subscriptions for the company’s AI platform will continue to increase. The company sells its AI-enhanced electrolytes and other enhanced battery materials. The company also sells lithium metal and high-silicon lithium-ion cells for drones and humanoid robotics. And finally, the company develops B-sample EV batteries. With the acquisition of UZ Energy, the company will receive revenue from energy storage services.

SES AI does not give updates on its specific operations, nor does it break down its revenue by source, except for contract licenses versus physical battery production. The company did not report any revenues for battery production for Q2-2025. Revenues came from service contracts. The company did not give any updates on its current JD partnerships. There are no updates on the company’s agreement with AISPEX in Texas to enhance its energy storage capabilities.

An analyst on Seeking Alpha recently interviewed the CEO of SES AI and shared some ideas on how the company operates. The company has B-sample batteries currently being tested by Hyundai and Honda, which are entering C-sample stage. SES does not plan to manufacture batteries, except for B samples or small batch manufacture. SES AI also offers its battery innovations to drone and robot makers for their batteries.

Revenue is made in different ways. Companies license Molecular Universe. SES AI works with specific vehicle and parts manufacturers to develop better batteries. The company uses its AI platform to manufacture new innovative battery cells and contracts out the manufacture. The operations from UZ Energy will also provide a revenue stream. The company’s next earnings report should include more details about its progress.

Q2-2025 Financial Performance

SES AI reported $3.5 million in revenue for Q2-2025, representing a 39% decrease QoQ. The company has only reported revenue for three quarters, so there is no pattern from previous performance. The company expects revenue to continue to increase.

Revenue comes from service contracts. The company has contracts with automotive OEM customers for the development of AI-enhanced lithium metal and lithium-ion battery materials. It also has contracts for the use of its AI platform.

Gross profit was $2.6 million, and gross margin was 74%. Because the company mostly licenses out services, its profit margin is high.

The company’s current operating expenses are far greater than its revenue. The company reported a net loss of $22.7 million.

Cash on hand has decreased because of the acquisition of UZ Energy. Cash and short-term investments were $228.9 million, a decrease QoQ and YoY. The company is currently debt-free.

SES AI shared its outlook in its earnings report. The company expects 2025 fiscal year revenues to be between $15 million and $25 million. The company has reported $9.3 million for 2025 so far.

Market consensus on the company’s next revenues for Q3-2025 is $4.58 million, an increase QoQ and YoY. The company reports in November.

SES AI has only reported revenues for three quarters. The company has high profit margins, but the amount is not enough to cover operating expenses. There is enough cash and ST investments for the company to operate another eight quarters at current expenses. The company’s assets far outweigh its liabilities.

The company’s stock price is overvalued. It trades above its book value per share and the median target price. High trading momentum and positive investor sentiment keep the price on an uptrend. The company’s stock enjoys 17.25% large institutional ownership. Investor sentiment is positive based on the future potential of the company. The company fits the current trend on the market. It is a small-cap AI play which just entering the revenue stage. The company forecasts the global energy storage market to be around $300 billion and the global EV market is valued around the same value.

There is a risk here because the investors are banking on future performance. The company will take time before it overcomes its quarterly net loss. Sooner or later, debt may occur to cover operations or future acquisitions.

Investment Strategy and Risk

Again, SES AI is at low risk of poor financial performance. The company’s assets outweigh its liabilities. Although it operates at a net loss, the company is increasing its revenue and revenue potential.

A Strong Buy comes at a moderate to high risk. The stock has recently rallied and may return to a lower price. Since revenues are lower than operating costs, investor sentiment may sour on the company. Overall market volatility may drop the price.

Conclusion

SES AI has moved into the revenue stage, and it has many synergies on the horizon. The company’s recent acquisition of UZ Energy allows it to integrate its AI systems and profit from the company’s current operations. SES AI is also making revenues from licensing out its AI platform to other battery developers. Investor sentiment remains positive on the company and its potential. While keeping the risks in mind, I continue my rating of a Strong Buy.

data.title: Record Options Activity Post FOMC Led By Call Buying
data.description:
By Mandy Xu

Cross-Asset Volatility: Implied volatilities declined across most asset classes last week, albeit modestly, following the Fed rate cut. Oil volatility declined the most, with WTI 1M implied vol down 1.2 vol pt to 28.3% as oil prices ended the week unch’d. Interest rate volatility (both MOVE and VIXTLT) fell to a 1-year low as consensus built around the rate path, with 2 additional cuts almost fully priced in for the rest of the year. Notably, while bond yields have declined significantly in recent months on hopes of Fed easing, inflation expectations have risen further, with 1Y inflation swaps now trading around 3.3% (vs. 2.5% in Jan). In other words, US real yields have collapsed with bond traders wagering the Fed will look through any inflation uptick and focus entirely on the softening labor market. Falling real yields explain why gold is the best performing asset YTD, with GLD up 40% YTD and gold volatility remaining the richest cross-asset vol (driven by upside call demand). See Exhibit 1.

Equity Volatility: “Spot up, vol up” was the theme in equity vol last week as SPX® fixed strike vols increased despite the market rally. As a result, the VIX® index ended the week up 0.7 pt to 15.5% even as realized volatility declined further. The SPX 1M implied-realized vol spread more than doubled from 2.2% to 5.0% (76th percentile high over the past year). Demand for optionality increased notably post-FOMC as option volumes surged, led by single stocks. Single stock option volume hit a record high of 54M contracts on Friday.

Skew: Most of the uptick in volume came from the call-side as the equity put/call ratio fell to a 3-months low of 0.45. The bullish sentiment can also be seen in the number of names trading with inverted call skew (i.e. OTM calls trading at a premium to ATM calls), which is typically a sign of extreme optimism. Historically, only ~3% of stocks in S&P top 100 trade with this characteristic at any given time. Over the past week, that number has surged from 3% to 12% (see Exhibit 2) – though it still pales to previous episodes of widespread investor euphoria (e.g. in 2021 or 4Q24 where that percentage reached a high of 30%). At the index level, what’s notable is the surge in bullish activity in the Russell 2000 index, with IWM call volumes hitting an all-time high of 2.85M contracts last Monday (over 5.4x the number of puts trading). See Exhibit 3. Not surprisingly, Russell skew flattened further last week, with 1M skew (25-delta ratio) now trading in the 15th percentile low over the past year.

Correlation & Dispersion: Stock dispersion increased last week on the back of the bid to single stock vol. VIXEQSM index gained over 2.5 vol pts while the DSPXSM index jumped back to 30%.

Cross-Asset Volatility Monitor

Cross-Asset Volatility Snapshot (10Y Lookback)

The chart shows how various asset class implied volatilities are trading relative to their own history, using a 10-year lookback.

The volatilities are ranked by z-scores, which compares the current volatility level for an asset versus its own 10- year history. A z-score of +2, for example, would mean that the current volatility level is trading 2 standard deviations above its long-term average (i.e. rich) while a z-score of -2 would mean it’s trading 2 standard deviations below average (i.e. cheap)

Because each asset class volatility trades at different levels and often defined differently (e.g. lognormal vs. normal vol, price vs. bps vol), by standardizing using z- scores, we’re able to compare different asset class volatilities on the same scale - and see at a glance if there are any divergences or dislocations in the cross- asset volatility markets

Cross-Asset Correlation Matrix (1M)

Cross-Asset Correlation Analysis

Macro Equity Volatility

VIX Index Volatility

US Index Volatility

Disclaimers:

There are important risks associated with transacting in any of the Cboe Company products discussed here. Before engaging in any transactions in those products, it is important for market participants to carefully review the disclosures and disclaimers contained at: Disclosures and Disclaimers Related to Cboe Options and Futures Products

These products are complex and are suitable only for sophisticated market participants. In certain jurisdictions, Cboe Company products are only permitted for investment professionals, certified sophisticated investors, or high net worth corporations and associations. These products involve the risk of loss, which can be substantial and, depending on the type of product, can exceed the amount of money deposited in establishing the position. Market participants should put at risk only funds that they can afford to lose without affecting their lifestyle.

data.title: Bakkt Joins Market Mania But Remains In Cash Burn Reality
data.description:
I've been bearish on Bakkt Holdings, Inc. (NYSE:BKKT) on the back of what's been consistent quarters of net losses, negative free cash flow ("FCF'), and a declining liquidity balance against its crypto operations that have not had a healthy stock market fit. However, in a new market zeitgeist where fundamentals seem to be simply suggestions to be replaced by vibes, BKKT has seen its common equity rally heavily in response to a deluge of positive news. The ticker is set to expand its board with the addition of Mike Alfred, an IREN Limited (IREN) board member and founder of Alpine Fox, a bitcoin-focused private investment fund. IREN owns and operates data centers for AI and mines Bitcoin ("BTC"). The stock rallied heavily on the news, moving to a more than 3-year high, perhaps with the view being that the addition of a new board member will be able to help reverse years of operational decline and compounding losses.

Akshay Naheta, who joined BKKT in March as co-CEO, was also named sole CEO and President. Naheta was previously a senior executive at SoftBank Group (OTCPK:SFTBY), leading on the company's public equity investments and working on some large tentpole deals, including an investment in Nvidia (NVDA) when he joined the Tokyo-based multinational investment company in 2017, before leaving in 2021 to create Distributed Technologies Research. He joined BKKT earlier this year. BKKT reported fiscal 2025 second-quarter revenue of $577.88 million, up 13% over its year-ago comp. Total operating expenses expanded by 12% year-over-year to come in at $596.37 million. Bulls would be right to highlight that this still drove a net loss of $14.7 million that was lower than the year-ago figure, with negative EPS down by 51 cents year-over-year. BKKT's trailing twelve-month FCF position is still negative, but the company is seeing an upswing.

Free Cash Flow, Liquidity, And New Operations

BKKT's second-quarter results are in some ways legacy financials. The company is going through a metamorphosis, divesting its loyalty business for $11 million in a definitive agreement set to close in the third quarter. Critically, the loyalty business, while broadly higher margin than BKKT's core crypto services revenue, was seeing year-over-year declines in revenue. Revenue for the segment during the second quarter dipped by 23% over its year-ago quarter. The money from the sale should help bolster a cash, cash equivalents, and restricted cash position, which stood at $61.45 million as of the end of the second quarter.

BKKT also completed the sale of Bakkt Trust to Intercontinental Exchange (ICE) for proceeds of around $4.5 million, generating a loss on the sale of around $2.3 million. The firm in transition also sold 6.75 million of its common shares and warrants post-period end in July to raise $75 million to further develop its BTC as a treasury strategy. The sales were of immaterial operations, and BKKT's highly unprofitable core crypto services business will remain the primary driver of its fundamentals.

Management communicated during the second quarter earnings call that their strategy will hinge on three pillars: (1) Brokerage-in-a-box; (2) Stablecoin payments; and (3) Bitcoin as a treasury. BKKT acquired the domain name ("bitcoin.co.jp") last week in a move management stated will help build the third pillar. While issuing a business wire for a domain name seems unnecessary, BKKT wants to signal to the market that it's a different company. This has underpinned the shift in vibes for a ticker that once had to engineer a 1-for-25 reverse stock split and whose liquidity has been weighed down by back-to-back quarters of negative FCF.

FCF also seems to be on an upswing, which, when aggregated with the recent asset sales, does provide a healthier fundamental backdrop for BKKT. Shareholders seem to be ignoring the dilution for now, with the BKKT also recently redeeming a $25 million outstanding 0.00% convertible debenture due June 18, 2026. BKKT always held little debt, with the recent move helping plump up liquidity. The primary risk continues to coalesce around the direction of FCF and just how deep a cash runway the equity offering, existing cash position, and the proceeds from the sale of the loyalty business will form.

Conclusion

Do I still think BKKT is a sell? This isn't clear. The ongoing rally could have some legs. The CEO will have to prove to the market that the transformation can stem historical cash burn and maintain healthy liquidity levels. Investing in BTC as part of a corporate treasury strategy only works until the market tips back bearish and focus again shifts to underlying fundamentals. I'm rating BKKT as a hold against the clearer shift in operations.

data.title: This Economic Report Spells Goldilocks
data.description:
Stocks took a breather yesterday, as Chairman Powell reminded investors that the Fed remains data dependent from one meeting to the next, and that there is no preset course for monetary policy, despite market expectations for another rate cut at each of the final two meetings of the year. He also noted that the risks to inflation are to the upside, while the risks to the labor market are to the downside, which means that there is “no risk-free path” for policy. His comments weighed on the major market indexes, but the selling looked more like profit taking after a record run rather than a concern about the outlook. To the contrary, I think yesterday’s only economic report spells Goldilocks for the economy.

The S&P Global Flash PMI (purchasing managers index) indicates that business activity slowed for a second consecutive month with the index falling from 54.6 in August to 53.6 in September, but it remains well into expansionary territory. The line of demarcation between expansion and contraction is 50. A softer rate of business activity suggests that the labor market will remain soft, which was corroborated by the sub-index for employment falling to a five-month low. That should keep the Fed in easing mode moving forward, even if it skips a rate cut at its October meeting.

At the same time, while input prices for producers purchasing materials rose to a four-month high, the sub-index for prices received from consumers of end products eased to its lowest level since April. In particular, goods price inflation cooled to the lowest level since January, which is not what I expected at this stage. This suggests that tariffs are still largely being absorbed by businesses and not passed on to consumers en masse. This will clearly take a toll on profit margins, but it will lessen the impact on consumer price inflation, which is the Fed’s primary concern.

A softening labor market combined with a more moderate increase in consumer prices than expected is as good as it gets for the investment landscape. Again, it is a Goldilocks backdrop for the US economy, because it appears that the rate of economic growth will sustain through this period of temporary price increases, allowing the Fed to continue easing monetary policy over the coming 12-month period to a neutral rate of interest. The S&P Global Composite PMI is just one report, but it has been one of the more reliable ones in the post-pandemic period, which is why it is one of my favorite real-time pulses on US economic activity.
